name: clusterfutures

metadata.name: clusterfutures
metadata.summary: futures for remote execution on clusters

documentation:
# Cluster Futures

This module provides a Python [concurrent.futures] executor that lets you run
functions on remote systems in your [HTCondor] or [Slurm] cluster. Stop worrying
about writing job files, scattering/gathering, and serialization---this module
does it all for you.

It uses the [cloudpickle] library to allow (most) closures to be used
transparently, so you're not limited to "pure" functions.

Installation:

```
pip install clusterfutures
```

Usage:

```python
import cfut
def square(n):
    return n * n

with cfut.SlurmExecutor() as executor:
    for result in executor.map(square, [5, 7, 11]):
        print(result)
```

See [slurm_example.py] and [condor_example.py] for further examples.
The easiest way to get started is to
ignore the fact that futures are being used at all and just use the provided
`map` function, which behaves like [itertools.imap] but transparently
distributes your work across the cluster.

## Goals & design

*clusterfutures* is a simple wrapper to run Python functions in batch jobs on
an HPC cluster. Each future corresponds to one batch job. The functions
that you run through clusterfutures should normally run for at least a few
seconds each: running smaller functions will be inefficient because of the
overhead of launching jobs and moving data.

Functions, parameters and return values are sent by creating files; this assumes
that the control process and the worker nodes have a shared filesystem.
This mechanism is convenient for relatively small amounts of data; it's probably
not the best way to transfer large amounts of data to & from workers.

[cloudpickle]: https://github.com/cloudpipe/cloudpickle
[concurrent.futures]: https://docs.python.org/3/library/concurrent.futures.html
[condor_example.py]: https://github.com/sampsyo/clusterfutures/blob/master/condor_example.py
[htcondor]: https://research.cs.wisc.edu/htcondor/
[itertools.imap]: https://docs.python.org/3/library/itertools.html#itertools.imap
[slurm]: https://slurm.schedmd.com/
[slurm_example.py]: https://github.com/sampsyo/clusterfutures/blob/master/slurm_example.py

modules.name: cfut.slurm
documentation: Abstracts access to a Slurm cluster via its command-line tools.

functions.name: cfut.slurm.submit_text
documentation:
Submits a Slurm job represented as a job file string. Returns
the job ID.

functions.name: cfut.slurm.submit
documentation: Starts a Slurm job that runs the specified shell command line.

functions.name: cfut.slurm.jobs_finished
documentation: Check which ones of the given Slurm jobs already finished

modules.name: cfut.condor
documentation: Abstracts access to a Condor cluster via its command-line tools.

functions.name: cfut.condor.submit_text
documentation:
Submits a Condor job represented as a job file string. Returns
the cluster ID of the submitted job.

functions.name: cfut.condor.submit
documentation:
Starts a Condor job based on specified parameters. A job
description is generated. Returns the cluster ID of the new job.

functions.name: cfut.condor.submit_script
documentation:
Like `submit` but takes the text of an executable script that
should be used instead of a filename. Returns the cluster ID along
with the name of the temporary script file executed. (This should
probably be removed once the job completes.)

functions.name: cfut.condor.wait
documentation: Waits for a cluster (or specific job) to complete.

functions.name: cfut.condor.getoutput
documentation:
Waits for a job to complete and then returns its standard output
and standard error data if the files were given default names.
Deletes these files after reading them if `cleanup` is True.

classes.name: cfut.condor.WaitThread

methods.name: cfut.condor.WaitThread.__init__
documentation:
The callable `callback` will be invoked with the cluster
ID of every waited-upon job that finishes. `interval`
specifies the polling rate.

methods.name: cfut.condor.WaitThread.stop
documentation: Stop the thread soon.

methods.name: cfut.condor.WaitThread.wait
documentation: Adds a new job ID to the set of jobs being waited upon.

A worker that polls Condor log files to observe when jobs
finish. Each cluster is only waited upon once (after which it is
"reaped" from the waiting pool).

functions.name: cfut.util.call
documentation:
Invokes a shell command as a subprocess, optionally with some
data sent to the standard input. Returns the standard output data,
the standard error, and the return code.

functions.name: cfut.util.chcall
documentation:
Like `call` but raises an exception when the return code is
nonzero. Only returns the stdout and stderr data.

functions.name: cfut.util.shlex_join
documentation: Return a shell-escaped string from *split_command*.
This is part of the shlex module from Python 3.8. This is copied from 3.10:

classes.name: cfut.util.CommandError
documentation: Raised when a shell command exits abnormally.

modules.name: cfut.remote
documentation: Tools for executing remote commands.

functions.name: cfut.remote.worker
documentation: Called to execute a job on a remote host.

modules.name: cfut
documentation: Python futures for Condor clusters.

functions.name: cfut.map
documentation:
Convenience function to map a function over cluster jobs. Given
a function and an iterable, generates results. (Works like
`itertools.imap`.) If `ordered` is False, then the values are
generated in an undefined order, possibly more quickly.

classes.name: cfut.FileWaitThread

methods.name: cfut.FileWaitThread.__init__
documentation:
The callable `callback` will be invoked with value
associated with the filename of each file that is created.
`interval` specifies the polling rate.

methods.name: cfut.FileWaitThread.stop
documentation: Stop the thread soon.

methods.name: cfut.FileWaitThread.wait
documentation:
Adds a new filename (and its associated callback value) to
the set of files being waited upon.

methods.name: cfut.FileWaitThread.check
documentation:
Do one check for completed jobs

The i parameter allows subclasses like SlurmWaitThread to do something
on every Nth check.

A thread that polls the filesystem waiting for a list of files to
be created. When a specified file is created, it invokes a callback.

classes.name: cfut.ClusterExecutor

methods.name: cfut.ClusterExecutor._start
documentation:
Start a job with the given worker ID and return an ID
identifying the new job. The job should run ``python -m
cfut.remote <workerid>.

methods.name: cfut.ClusterExecutor._cleanup
documentation:
Given a job ID as returned by _start, perform any necessary
cleanup after the job has finished.

methods.name: cfut.ClusterExecutor._completion
documentation: Called whenever a job finishes.

methods.name: cfut.ClusterExecutor.submit
documentation:
Submit a job to the pool.

If additional_setup_lines is passed, it overrides the lines given
when creating the executor.

methods.name: cfut.ClusterExecutor.shutdown
documentation: Close the pool.
An abstract base class for executors that run jobs on clusters.

classes.name: cfut.SlurmExecutor
documentation:
Futures executor for executing jobs on a Slurm cluster.

additional_setup_lines is a list of lines to include in the shell script
passed to sbatch. They may include sbatch options (starting with
'#SBATCH') and shell commands, e.g. to set environment variables.

classes.name: cfut.CondorExecutor
documentation: Futures executor for executing jobs on a Condor cluster.
